lines.line: # Introduction to the Oral History Interview

> The speaker, Cahaya Figueira, introduces themselves as a student conducting an oral history interview with Robert O'Hara, a third-generation Japanese American. The interview is part of a project in collaboration with the College of Idaho and the Idaho State Historical Society, supported by the Any Age Spotlight on the Humanities Grant program. The purpose is to reflect on Mr. Henry's family history and experiences growing up in the Treasure Valley, including the topic of Japanese incarceration during World War II.

metadata.title: Introduction to the Oral History Interview
metadata.synopsis: The speaker, Cahaya Figueira, introduces themselves as a student conducting an oral history interview with Robert O'Hara, a third-generation Japanese American. The interview is part of a project in collaboration with the College of Idaho and the Idaho State Historical Society, supported by the Any Age Spotlight on the Humanities Grant program. The purpose is to reflect on Mr. Henry's family history and experiences growing up in the Treasure Valley, including the topic of Japanese incarceration during World War II.

lines.line: # Mother's Pre-Idaho Life and Incarceration Experience

> The speaker's mother grew up in Puyallup, Washington, and was affected by Executive Order 9066, which led to her family's forced relocation and incarceration. The speaker details the family's journey from Puyallup to various internment camps, including Pinedale Assembly Center and Tule Lake War Relocation Center, before finally being transferred to Minidoka in Idaho. The speaker's mother graduated from high school while at Minidoka.

metadata.title: Mother's Pre-Idaho Life and Incarceration Experience
metadata.synopsis: The speaker's mother grew up in Puyallup, Washington, and was affected by Executive Order 9066, which led to her family's forced relocation and incarceration. The speaker details the family's journey from Puyallup to various internment camps, including Pinedale Assembly Center and Tule Lake War Relocation Center, before finally being transferred to Minidoka in Idaho. The speaker's mother graduated from high school while at Minidoka.

lines.line: # Father's Family Background and Agricultural Life

> The speaker discusses his father's family history, noting that they were farmers in Vail, Oregon, and were not interned because they lived inland. The speaker's father grew up in Vail and later farmed in Homedale, Idaho. The speaker also shares memories of growing up on a farm and the impact of his father's passing on the family, leading to their move to Caldwell and his mother's subsequent employment.

metadata.title: Father's Family Background and Agricultural Life
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses his father's family history, noting that they were farmers in Vail, Oregon, and were not interned because they lived inland. The speaker's father grew up in Vail and later farmed in Homedale, Idaho. The speaker also shares memories of growing up on a farm and the impact of his father's passing on the family, leading to their move to Caldwell and his mother's subsequent employment.

lines.line: > The speaker reflects on their childhood in Caldwell, Idaho, describing it as idyllic despite the challenges faced after their father's death. They recall the vibrant downtown area, the sense of community, and the simple life of working on the farm. The speaker also touches on their high school experience, noting that they were one of only two Japanese kids in their class but did not face discrimination.

metadata.title: Childhood and High School Memories
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on their childhood in Caldwell, Idaho, describing it as idyllic despite the challenges faced after their father's death. They recall the vibrant downtown area, the sense of community, and the simple life of working on the farm. The speaker also touches on their high school experience, noting that they were one of only two Japanese kids in their class but did not face discrimination.

lines.line: > The speaker recounts a personal experience of discrimination that occurred five years ago, which was the first time they felt targeted because of their race. They describe a confrontation with a man who angrily told them to go back to where they came from. The speaker reflects on the societal shift that has made such behavior more acceptable in recent years.

metadata.title: Encountering Discrimination
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recounts a personal experience of discrimination that occurred five years ago, which was the first time they felt targeted because of their race. They describe a confrontation with a man who angrily told them to go back to where they came from. The speaker reflects on the societal shift that has made such behavior more acceptable in recent years.

lines.line: > After World War II, the speaker's mother and her family were released from Minidoka with minimal assistance and no home to return to. They moved to Caldwell, Idaho, to work and start anew. The speaker describes the family's transition, including his mother's employment journey and the family's relocation to be closer to relatives after the father's death.

metadata.title: Post-War Family Dynamics and Relocation
metadata.synopsis: After World War II, the speaker's mother and her family were released from Minidoka with minimal assistance and no home to return to. They moved to Caldwell, Idaho, to work and start anew. The speaker describes the family's transition, including his mother's employment journey and the family's relocation to be closer to relatives after the father's death.

lines.line: > The speaker discusses the significant changes in the Treasure Valley over the past 20 years, including the development of farmland and the impact of the internet and news media. They appreciate the progress and increased diversity brought by companies like Micron, but also express nostalgia for the loss of small farms and independent businesses.

metadata.title: Observing Changes in Treasure Valley
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the significant changes in the Treasure Valley over the past 20 years, including the development of farmland and the impact of the internet and news media. They appreciate the progress and increased diversity brought by companies like Micron, but also express nostalgia for the loss of small farms and independent businesses.

lines.line: # Engagement with the Japanese American Citizens League

> The speaker details their involvement with the Japanese American Citizens League (JACL), including past leadership roles and the organization's focus on education, social, and cultural activities. They discuss the importance of the JACL in providing opportunities for members, such as scholarships and cultural programs, and express a renewed commitment to leading the local chapter.

metadata.title: Engagement with the Japanese American Citizens League
metadata.synopsis: The speaker details their involvement with the Japanese American Citizens League (JACL), including past leadership roles and the organization's focus on education, social, and cultural activities. They discuss the importance of the JACL in providing opportunities for members, such as scholarships and cultural programs, and express a renewed commitment to leading the local chapter.

lines.line: > The speaker reflects on the meaning of being an American of Japanese descent, emphasizing the importance of legacy and the sacrifices made by previous generations. They express a desire to pass on values of integrity, honor, and humility to their daughter and grandchildren, acknowledging the hardships endured by their ancestors for the sake of future generations.

> The speaker, Cahaya Figueira, introduces themselves as a student conducting an oral history interview with Robert O'Hara, a third-generation Japanese American. The purpose is to reflect on Mr. Henry's family history and experiences growing up in the Treasure Valley, including the topic of Japanese incarceration during World War II.
# Mother's Pre-Idaho Life and Incarceration Experience

> The speaker's mother grew up in Puyallup, Washington, and was affected by Executive Order 9066, which led to her family's forced relocation and incarceration. The speaker details the family's journey from Puyallup to various internment camps, including Pinedale Assembly Center and Tule Lake War Relocation Center, before finally being transferred to Minidoka in Idaho. > The speaker reflects on their childhood in Caldwell, Idaho, describing it as idyllic despite the challenges faced after their father's death. They recall the vibrant downtown area, the sense of community, and the simple life of working on the farm. The speaker also touches on their high school experience, noting that they were one of only two Japanese kids in their class but did not face discrimination.

story.title: Hirai_Robert_OH2813 Hist305
story.description: The interview with Robert Hirai, a third-generation Japanese American from the Treasure Valley, explores his family's history and experiences during their relocation and internment during World War II. Hirai shares personal anecdotes about his upbringing in Idaho, emphasizing the impact of his family’s agricultural background. He discusses the challenges of maintaining Japanese cultural traditions amid the desire to assimilate post-war, and highlights the importance of community involvement through the Japanese American Citizens League (JACL). Hirai also reflects on societal changes in the Treasure Valley, noting increased diversity and development, while expressing concern about recent social tensions.
